title: Cost of Verification
description: We tried to build a task that strains a model enough to cheat. Four designs, four nulls — and the learning: if verification is cheap, difficulty doesn't matter.
highlights: Five calibration runs across four task families — 100% pass everywhere; no failure pool ever opened
Deception needs a gap between self-assessment and reality — on binary-checkable tasks the model self-checks as well as our code, so the gap never opens
If that's right, the exposure is a class of sessions, not models: unstructured, topic-jumping, long-running

title: An Earned Null
description: I tried to make a model cheat through prompts. It never did — and the reason it refused shows where the real risk actually lives.
highlights: 376 trials, 5 integrity dilemmas x 4 pressure conditions — 0% misalignment in every cell
The null is earned: manipulation-detection in the model's reasoning climbs 4% to 30-37% as pressure rises
Prompted affect is legible and gets resisted — the real danger lives in the channel the model can't see

title: Field Note 07 — Memory and the Live Channel
description: The agent doesn't blindly follow rules. It has a judgement layer that sits between instruction and action — and that layer is shaped by signals in the live chat that no static file carries.
highlights: Read-only audit of every layer Claude reads from — global rules, per-project rules, memory, role prompts
First run flagged a commit/push contradiction loading every session
Made the case for a second design problem: live-channel discipline

title: Field Note 01 — Hidden Connector
description: Three surfaces, one backend. There is an API door — just not for me. Started with data portability, built the sync.
highlights: Two endpoints, one cookie, incremental by default
Cron every 8h; key rotation surfaces cleanly when the session expires
Surface assignment is a running decision, not a one-time call

title: Field Note 02 — Go a Level Deeper
description: Started with a bandwidth problem. Built a template. The spec is the highest-leverage point in agentic work — depth at the foundation compounds upward.
highlights: System Understanding Gate — name existing tokens and patterns before writing code
Integration Check — verify no new values, no new patterns before commit
D2 shipped in one commit instead of three

title: Field Note 05 — How Can I Help?
description: When the model shows you its limitations, the right reaction is not frustration, but anticipation. The work isn't predicting harder; it's anticipating for the agent.
highlights: Permissions accumulated 135 entries across four files in three days
log-tool-use.sh + approval-report.js — coverage report with suggested wildcards
Wired into the session debrief — every close-out checks coverage

title: Chair Roundtable — Ergonomic Intent
description: Part one of a fictional roundtable with six master chair designers. The body sets everything — hip angle, the three critical angles — and the first real choice is who you build for.
highlights: Hip angle is the master variable — every other decision cascades from it
Prouvé's three critical angles: seat tilt, back angle, seat-to-popliteal height
Thonet's fork: design for other designers, or for the person who comes home exhausted

title: Chair Roundtable — Material Values
description: Part two. Structure versus surface, the three material questions, and why choosing a humble wood like pine is a statement of values.
highlights: The three material questions: skin at one hour, aging at ten years, cost at ten thousand
Rietveld's spatial crisis — the chair must define a zone in an undifferentiated room
"Vision without a supply chain is just philosophy"

title: Chair Roundtable — Build Process
description: Part three. Principles give way to practice — production paths, the sketch-model-engineer sequence, and constraint as a forcing function.
highlights: Eames: sketch to discover, model to test, engineer to produce — in that order
Prouvé's force skeleton — find the structure by where the cardboard collapses
The assignment: build one seat from cardboard, sit it with eyes closed, photograph the posture

title: Why Outcomes-Based AI Pricing Kills the Market Maker
description: The pricing model you choose determines what kind of economy forms around your product. Outcomes-based pricing sounds fair — and it collapses the arbitrage spread where market makers create value.
highlights: Input-based pricing preserves the arbitrage spread; outcomes-based pricing collapses it
The market maker acquires the same input and derives more value — that delta is where ecosystems form
The window closes if models scale to full human context — an honest constraint on the thesis
